chunk_summary: Time-bound ALT monitoring in pediatric MASLD involves structured follow-up, response assessment, and escalation strategies, including lifestyle changes, pharmacotherapy, and elastography for persistent ALT elevation or comorbidities.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ALT monitoring in pediatric MASLD diagnosis and management.
Learn time-bound criteria for defining response and nonresponse to lifestyle interventions.
Identify escalation strategies, including pharmacotherapy and elastography, for nonresponders.
Recognize sex-specific ALT thresholds and their clinical significance.
key_facts: Sex-specific ALT thresholds: >26 IU/L for boys and >22 IU/L for girls in adolescents.
Recheck ALT at 12 weeks post-intervention; assess response over 3–6 months.
Nonresponse: Persistent ALT >2× threshold or worsening trends despite lifestyle changes.
Escalation includes increased visit frequency, pharmacotherapy, or bariatric referral for class 2–3 obesity with comorbidities.
Elastography is recommended for persistent ALT elevation, multi-comorbidity clustering (e.g., T2DM), or hepatomegaly.
reference_quote: Use sex-specific pediatric upper limits of normal for ALT (about 26 IU/L for boys and 22 IU/L for girls in adolescents); treat values >2× these thresholds as clinically significant.
document_key_topics: Time-bound ALT monitoring in pediatric MASLD
Criteria for response and nonresponse in MASLD
